exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Low Bar Squat with Rack
exercises.notes: Set the rack height so the bar sits at about mid-chest when standing; load the bar and secure collars.
instructions: Set the rack height so the bar sits at about mid-chest when standing; load the bar and secure collars.
Step under the bar and position it low across the rear shoulders / lower trapezius (just below the top of the posterior delts), hands gripping the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar by straightening the legs, then take 1–2 small steps back to clear the rack and plant your feet shoulder-width to slightly wider with toes turned out about 10–30 degrees.
Brace your core and maintain a neutral spine and upright chest with eyes forward; squeeze the shoulder blades and keep the elbows slightly down and back to create a solid shelf for the bar.
Initiate the descent by pushing the hips back and bending the knees simultaneously, keeping knees tracking over the toes and weight distributed through the midfoot and heels.
Lower until the thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to your comfortable depth while keeping a neutral spine), maintaining the low-bar placement and tight torso throughout.
Drive upward by pressing through the heels/midfoot, extending the hips and knees together while keeping the chest up and core braced.
When standing tall at the top, stabilize the bar, step forward to the rack, and carefully re-rack the bar onto the rack supports.
exercises.description: A compound lower-body squat performed with the barbell set low across the rear deltoids/traps using a rack; it develops thigh strength while recruiting the posterior chain and core for stability. The low-bar position shifts some load onto the hips and upper back compared with a high-bar squat.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; feet about hip-width apart.
instructions: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; feet about hip-width apart.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip, arms relaxed; optionally lightly touch a wall or railing for balance (wall-assisted if needed).
Keep the torso upright, chest lifted and gaze forward; maintain a slight bend in the knees (do not lock them).
Inhale, then exhale and contract your calves to raise your heels as high as possible until you are standing on your toes; keep weight evenly distributed across the forefoot.
Pause at the top for 1–2 seconds, focusing on a full contraction of the calves while maintaining core tension.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels below the level of the step until you feel a controlled stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, controlling both the concentric (raise) and eccentric (lower) phases and breathing rhythmically.
exercises.description: A weighted standing calf raise performed on the edge of a step to load the calf muscles through a full plantarflexion and stretch. Dumbbells add resistance and grip demand while the core and glutes stabilize the body.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Weighted Chains Bench Squat
exercises.notes: Set a flat bench behind you at a height that allows your thighs to reach roughly parallel to the floor when seated; place a barbell with chains across your upper traps (barbell resting on the shoulders, not the neck).
instructions: Set a flat bench behind you at a height that allows your thighs to reach roughly parallel to the floor when seated; place a barbell with chains across your upper traps (barbell resting on the shoulders, not the neck).
Stand under the bar in a rack, grip the bar just outside shoulder width, take a deep breath and brace your abs and spine before unracking.
Step the bar out of the rack and take 1–2 controlled steps back until you are standing with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes angled slightly outwards; keep the bench directly behind you.

Begin the descent by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, keeping your chest up and spine neutral; keep knees tracking over your toes and weight distributed through the mid-foot and heels.
Lower in a controlled manner until your glutes lightly contact the bench (do not fully sit or relax on the bench; maintain tension), ensuring thighs reach about parallel to the floor.

Pause briefly on the bench (no bouncing), then drive through your heels and mid-foot, extend hips and knees to stand up explosively while exhaling; allow the chains to rise and add resistance as you near lockout.
At the top, fully extend hips and knees while maintaining a braced core and neutral spine; reset your breath as needed between reps.
After completing the set, carefully walk the bar forward and re-rack it in the squat rack.

Coaching notes: keep the bar on the upper traps (not high on the neck), avoid letting knees collapse inward, control the descent to protect knees and lower back, and adjust bench height or foot position to maintain proper depth and comfort.
exercises.description: A barbell back squat performed to a bench with chains added for accommodating resistance; this bench-squat variation emphasizes the quadriceps while using the bench as a depth cue. Chains increase load as you rise, encouraging stronger lockout.

exercises.name: Barbell Sumo Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a squat-like setup (upright torso, hips lower than a conventional deadlift) and grip the bar with hands inside your knees using a double overhand or mixed grip.
instructions: Stand with feet well outside shoulder width (sumo stance), toes pointed slightly outward; position the bar over the mid-foot while standing upright.

Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a squat-like setup (upright torso, hips lower than a conventional deadlift) and grip the bar with hands inside your knees using a double overhand or mixed grip.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine (no rounded back); pull your shoulder blades down and back so the lats are engaged and chest is up.
Drive your knees out so they track over your toes (toes and knees aligned) to engage the adductors and maintain the wide stance.
Initiate the lift by pushing through the heels and mid-foot, driving the hips forward and straightening the legs simultaneously—keep the bar close to the shins.
Stand tall at lockout by fully extending the hips and knees, squeezing the glutes and keeping the shoulders back (avoid hyperextending the lower back).
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips first, then bending the knees to return the bar to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine and tight core.
Reset your position between reps as needed (re-brace core, realign knees over toes, and ensure bar remains over mid-foot) before initiating the next repetition.
exercises.description: The barbell sumo deadlift is a wide-stance deadlift variation that emphasizes hip extension and inner-thigh drive to lift the bar from the floor. It uses a more upright torso and wider foot placement to shift load onto the glutes and adductors while still recruiting the posterior chain and legs.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Step-up
exercises.notes: Stand sideways beside a bench with feet roughly hip-width apart and a dumbbell in each hand held at your sides (neutral grip).
instructions: Stand sideways beside a bench with feet roughly hip-width apart and a dumbbell in each hand held at your sides (neutral grip).
Position the foot closest to the bench flat on the center of the bench so your knee tracks over your toes; the other foot remains on the floor.
Brace your abs, keep your chest upright and core tight, then drive through the heel of the elevated foot to extend the knee and hip and lift your body up onto the bench.
Stand tall on the bench, squeezing the quads and glutes at the top; avoid rotating or leaning the torso away from the bench.
Control the descent: step the trailing foot back down to the floor first or lower the lead foot down with control so the same side returns to the start position.
Repeat for the prescribed reps on one side, then switch to the other leg and perform the same number of reps.
exercises.description: A single‑leg step-up performed sideways onto a bench while holding dumbbells, emphasizing knee and hip extension of the stepping leg to raise the body laterally. It develops unilateral leg strength, balance and hip stability with emphasis on the thighs and glutes.

exercises.name: Lever Lying Single Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the lever pad sits just above the back of your ankle and the pivot aligns with your knee joint.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the lever pad sits just above the back of your ankle and the pivot aligns with your knee joint.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with hips and pelvis firmly against the pad, one foot hooked under the padded lever and the other leg extended and relaxed.
Grasp the handles or edge of the bench for stability and brace your core to prevent your hips from lifting.
Start with the working leg nearly straight (slight natural bend in the knee) and exhale as you flex the knee to curl the heel toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause briefly at the top when you feel a full hamstring contraction without excessive hip extension.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start position, fully controlling the eccentric phase without letting the weight slam down.
Keep the non-working leg relaxed and hips pressed into the bench; avoid lifting or thrusting the hips to cheat the movement.
Complete the desired reps, then switch legs and repeat for the other side.
exercises.description: Prone (lying face down) single-leg curl performed on a lever/lying leg curl machine that isolates the hamstrings by flexing the knee to bring the heel toward the glutes. Movement emphasizes a controlled concentric curl and a slow eccentric return while keeping the hips pinned to the bench.

exercises.name: Lever Lying Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine so the pivot aligns with the knee joint and place the lower-leg pad just above the heels / over the Achilles area.
instructions: Set the lever machine so the pivot aligns with the knee joint and place the lower-leg pad just above the heels / over the Achilles area.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with your hips flat on the pad, chest supported, and hands holding the front handles or bench for stability.
Position your legs fully extended with the pad resting on the back of your lower legs and feet relaxed; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Exhale and curl your lower legs by flexing the knees, bringing your heels toward your glutes in a controlled motion until you reach near-full knee flexion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top for 1 second, keeping hips and torso pressed into the bench (avoid lifting the hips).
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start (near full knee extension) under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full range of motion; adjust load so you can control both concentric and eccentric phases.
Stop or reduce weight if you feel excessive hip rise, lower-back strain, or knee pain; keep movements smooth and joint-focused.
exercises.description: Prone (lying face down) leg curl performed on a lever machine that isolates the hamstrings by flexing the knee against a padded lever. The movement curls the lower leg toward the buttocks while the torso remains stabilized on the bench.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright on the machine platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; position your shoulders under the padded lever and grasp the handles.
instructions: Stand upright on the machine platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; position your shoulders under the padded lever and grasp the handles.
Unlatch or release the safety so the weight rests on the shoulders and stand tall with a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (not locked).
Inhale, then exhale as you press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing the calves at the top.
Hold the top position briefly (1 second) while maintaining an upright torso and stable hips.
Inhale as you slowly lower your heels down below the platform level until you feel a stretch in the calves.
Control the descent—avoid bouncing—and repeat for desired reps, then carefully re-rack or secure the lever when finished.
exercises.description: A bilateral standing calf raise performed on a lever/shoulder machine that isolates the calf muscles through plantarflexion. The exercise raises the heels off a platform, then lowers them below platform level for a full stretch.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell in a rack at about shoulder height.
instructions: Set the barbell in a rack at about shoulder height. Step under the bar and position it across the front of your shoulders (front of delts/upper chest) using either a clean (front-rack) grip or a cross-arm hold.
Stand up to unrack the bar, take one or two small steps back, and place your feet about shoulder-width apart with toes pointed slightly out.
Raise your elbows so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and brace your abs to keep the torso upright throughout the movement.
Inhale, push your hips back slightly and bend at the knees to descend into a squat; keep your chest up, elbows high, and knees tracking over your toes.
Continue lowering until your thighs reach at least parallel to the floor (or slightly below if mobility allows) while maintaining a neutral spine.
Exhale and drive through your heels and midfoot to extend your knees and hips, returning to the standing start position with control.
Maintain tight abs and high elbows on every rep; avoid letting the knees cave inward or the torso fold forward. Rack the bar safely when finished.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat where the barbell is held across the front of the shoulders, emphasizing upright torso and knee extension to target the quadriceps. Performed by descending into a squat and driving back up while keeping elbows high and core braced.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set up a barbell in a squat rack at about shoulder height and load appropriate weight; step under the bar and place it across your upper traps (back-squat position), hands gripping the bar for stability.
instructions: Set up a barbell in a squat rack at about shoulder height and load appropriate weight; step under the bar and place it across your upper traps (back-squat position), hands gripping the bar for stability.
Stand with the balls of your feet on a raised platform, plate, or step so your heels can drop below the platform; feet should be hip-width and toes pointing forward.
Unrack the bar and step clear of the rack, keeping your torso tall, chest up and core braced.
Exhale and press through the balls of both feet to raise your heels as high as possible, keeping knees straight but not locked and avoiding forward lean.
Pause and squeeze the calves at the top for a second.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels down below the platform level until you feel a stretch in the calves; control the descent—do not bounce.
Repeat for the desired reps, then carefully step back into the rack to re-rack the bar.
Common cues: keep a neutral spine, maintain steady tempo, breathe out on the lift and in on the lower, and use a spotter or safety pins if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: Standing barbell calf raises are a weighted heel-raise exercise performed with a barbell across the upper back to strengthen and develop the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves). The movement raises the heels up onto the balls of the feet and then lowers back down for a full calf contraction and stretch.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Step-up
exercises.notes: Start standing sideways next to a bench or sturdy platform with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip; stand close enough so the outer edge of your foot can step onto the bench.
instructions: Start standing sideways next to a bench or sturdy platform with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip; stand close enough so the outer edge of your foot can step onto the bench.
Brace your core (neutral spine and engaged abs), keep chest up and shoulders back, and gaze forward throughout the movement.
Place the foot closest to the bench onto the center of the bench with the entire foot contacting the surface, keeping the stepping knee tracking over the toes.

Drive through the heel of the foot on the bench and extend the hip and knee of that leg to lift your body up until the stepping leg is straight and you are standing tall on the bench (you may bring the trailing foot up to meet the lead foot or keep it hovering depending on your variation).
Pause briefly at the top with hips fully extended and weight primarily on the stepping leg; maintain upright posture and avoid leaning away from the bench.
Slowly lower your trailing foot back down to the floor in a controlled manner, then step the lead foot down to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps on one side, then switch sides so the other leg performs the step-ups. Focus on using the quads and glutes to lift, not momentum.
exercises.description: A lateral step-up performed while holding dumbbells at your sides; you step sideways onto a bench or platform to work the thighs and hip musculature. The exercise emphasizes single-leg pushing, targeting the quads while the glutes, hamstrings and stabilizers assist.

exercises.name: Barbell Dimel Deadlift
exercises.notes: Slightly bend the knees (soft knee) and brace your core, maintaining a neutral spine and chest up throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar with both hands just outside the legs using an overhand or mixed grip.
Slightly bend the knees (soft knee) and brace your core, maintaining a neutral spine and chest up throughout the movement.
Initiate the movement by pushing the hips back (hip hinge) and lower the barbell down the front of your legs toward mid-shin while keeping the bar close to your body.
Keep the neck neutral, shoulders retracted, and do not let the lower back round as you descend; feel a stretch in the hamstrings.
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward explosively while pulling the bar up along the thighs to a near-lockout (stand tall), squeezing the glutes at the top.
Control the descent and repeat for the desired reps, breathing in on the lower and exhaling as you drive the hips forward.
Use lighter weight and higher reps if performing Dimel-style sets, and stop or reduce range if you feel lower-back rounding or pain.
exercises.description: A barbell hip-hinge exercise performed from a standing position that emphasizes eccentric and concentric work of the hamstrings by lowering the bar toward the shins and driving the hips forward to stand. The movement trains the posterior chain with a short range of motion and controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Barbell Pause Deadlift
exercises.notes: Set position by hinging at the hips with a slight knee bend so your shins are near vertical, chest lifted, shoulder blades over the bar, and back neutral (no rounding).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell positioned over the mid-foot; approach the bar and grip it just outside the knees (double overhand or mixed grip).
Set position by hinging at the hips with a slight knee bend so your shins are near vertical, chest lifted, shoulder blades over the bar, and back neutral (no rounding).
Take a deep breath and brace your core, pull the slack out of the bar by tensioning the lats and upper back before initiating the lift.
Drive through the heels and extend the hips and knees together to lift the bar, keeping the bar close to the body and maintaining a neutral spine.
When the bar reaches the hip/knee region (the sticking point), intentionally pause and hold the top of the hinge for 1–2 seconds while keeping hips tight and chest up.
After the pause, actively push the hips back and bend the knees to lower the bar under control to the floor, maintaining a neutral spine throughout the descent.
Reset your position and breathing between reps if needed; avoid using a rounded back or excessive knee collapse during the lift and pause.
exercises.description: A barbell deadlift variation where the lifter pauses at the hip/knee region to remove momentum and increase time under tension, emphasizing hip extension. The pause forces greater glute and posterior chain activation while demanding stable posture.

exercises.name: Lever Deadlift (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load plates on the machine and release any safety latch so the lever can move through its full range.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width under the lever platform, toes pointing forward or slightly out; approach the machine so the handles are reachable when you hinge.
Load plates on the machine and release any safety latch so the lever can move through its full range.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees, push hips back, keep chest lifted and eyes forward so the torso is braced (neutral spine).
Reach down and grasp the machine handles with a firm, even grip; keep shoulders over or slightly in front of the handles.
Brace your core and take a full breath, then drive through the heels and extend the hips and knees simultaneously to stand the lever up.
At the top, lock the hips by squeezing the glutes and avoid excessive lumbar extension — stand tall but neutral.
Lower the weight by pushing the hips back first, then bending the knees to return the lever to the start position while maintaining a neutral spine and the bar close to the legs.
Control the descent to maintain tension in the hamstrings; inhale on the way down and exhale as you drive up.
Repeat for the desired reps, and re-secure the safety latch before unloading plates or stepping away from the machine.
exercises.description: A plate-loaded lever deadlift is a hip‑hinge dominant lift performed on a lever machine that trains posterior chain strength by extending the hips and knees from a lowered position. It emphasizes hamstrings and glute activation while requiring spinal stabilization and grip strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Good Morning
exercises.notes: Unrack the bar and step back, bracing your core: take a deep breath and maintain a neutral spine and a slight bend in the knees (soft knees).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, toes pointing forward, barbell placed securely across the upper back/traps (not on the neck), hands gripping the bar just outside the shoulders.
Unrack the bar and step back, bracing your core: take a deep breath and maintain a neutral spine and a slight bend in the knees (soft knees).

Initiate the movement by pushing the hips straight back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the torso to hinge forward until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings or the torso is near parallel to the floor.
Keep the neck neutral (eyes forward or slightly down), shoulders retracted, and avoid rounding the lower back throughout the hinge.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then drive the hips forward to return to the upright starting position by contracting the hamstrings and glutes while exhaling.
Perform the desired number of reps with controlled tempo; use lighter weight if you cannot maintain a neutral spine or proper hip hinge mechanics.
exercises.description: A hip-hinge movement performed with a barbell across the upper back that emphasizes the hamstrings while recruiting the glutes and spinal erectors to control torso position. It strengthens the posterior chain and challenges core stability.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand. Plant your feet flat on the floor and lie back so your head, upper back, and butt are supported (supine on incline bench).
Hold the dumbbells with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and press them together over your upper chest with a small bend in both elbows; this is the start position.
Keep your shoulder blades lightly retracted and braced throughout the set to protect the shoulder joint.

Lower one dumbbell in a slow, controlled arc out to the side (maintain the same elbow bend) until you feel a stretch in the upper chest; keep the opposite arm stationary and slightly elevated above the chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom for tension on the pectorals, then drive that dumbbell back up in the same arc to meet the elevated dumbbell above the chest, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Alternate sides each repetition, maintaining control, a constant elbow angle, and avoiding bouncing or letting the lowered elbow drop behind the plane of your torso.
Breathe in as you lower the weight and exhale as you bring it back up. Use a weight that allows strict form and full control for the full range of motion.
Avoid shrugging the shoulders or overarching the lower back; stop immediately if you feel sharp shoulder pain and reduce range or load.
exercises.description: Lying supine on an incline bench, perform alternating single-arm dumbbell flyes in a wide arc to isolate the upper chest (pectorals) while one arm remains elevated as the other lowers. The movement emphasizes a controlled stretch and contraction of the upper chest with stabilization from the anterior delts and forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise (support head)
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and let the arms hang straight down beneath your shoulders; maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Begin the movement by initiating at the shoulders: raise both dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc with a slight bend in the elbows, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue lifting until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (or in line with your shoulders), keeping the wrists neutral and avoiding excessive elbow lock.
Pause and squeeze the rear delts/upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the head and torso supported on the bench.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control, resisting any tendency to let momentum drop the weights.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower. Maintain a stable torso—do not rotate, jerk, or shrug the shoulders during the movement.
Use lighter weight if you cannot perform the motion with controlled form or if you feel neck or lumbar strain despite the head support.
exercises.description: A bent-over rear lateral raise performed with the head supported on an incline bench to isolate the posterior delts; lift dumbbells out to the sides while keeping the torso stable to target the rear shoulder and upper back. This variation reduces momentum and neck strain by using the bench for support.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Cable Half Kneeling Face Pull
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to upper‑chest/head height and attach a rope or double handle. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to upper‑chest/head height and attach a rope or double handle. Choose a light‑to‑moderate load to maintain strict form.
Assume a half‑kneeling position: one knee on the floor and the opposite foot planted forward so torso is upright and hips square to the pulley. Keep a tall spine and neutral neck.
Reach forward and grasp the rope/handles with a neutral or thumbs‑up grip. Start with arms extended and tension on the cable.
Brace your core and pull the handles toward your face by driving the elbows out to the sides while retracting the shoulder blades. Lead the movement with the elbows, not the hands.
At the end of the pull, externally rotate the shoulders so the hands/rope move toward the sides of your face or slightly behind the ears and squeeze the rear delts and upper back for 1 second.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, extending the arms back to the start while maintaining an upright torso and not collapsing the chest.
Perform all reps on one kneeling side, then switch legs and repeat to maintain unilateral balance. Breathe out while pulling, inhale while returning.
Keep cues in mind: avoid shrugging the shoulders, do not lean back to drive the weight, and use a weight that allows full external rotation and a controlled tempo.
exercises.description: A unilateral, half‑kneeling variation of the cable face pull that emphasizes the rear delts and upper back while adding core and hip stability demands. Pull the cable toward the face with elbows high and externally rotate the hands at the end of the pull to maximize rear delt activation.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about chest/shoulder height and attach single hand grips; select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about chest/shoulder height and attach single hand grips; select an appropriate weight.

Stand in the middle of the station facing away from the stacks with one foot slightly forward (staggered stance) for stability, arms bent and handles held at chest level, elbows roughly at chest height.
Brace your core (engage abs), keep a neutral spine and shoulders slightly retracted to set the starting position; wrists remain neutral.
Step forward or press your feet into the floor to create tension in the cables so the handles cannot move without effort.
Exhale and press both handles forward in a controlled motion, extending the arms until nearly straight but without locking the elbows; keep the movement horizontal and drive from the chest.
At full extension, pause briefly and squeeze the pectorals (and serratus anterior) while keeping the torso stable and not overextending the lower back.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with control, allowing the elbows to bend to chest level while maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping consistent tempo and avoiding excessive leaning or rotating of the torso.
exercises.description: A standing cable press performed from a dual‑pulley station that emphasizes horizontal pushing to work the chest. The movement also requires shoulder and triceps involvement while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Lever Crossovers
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees at about 90°.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-down grip and start with your arms opened out to the sides at shoulder height, elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your torso stable; avoid leaning forward or arching the lower back.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a slow, controlled arc in front of your chest, allowing the hands to cross slightly if the machine path allows.
Pause briefly at full contraction and focus on squeezing the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position, maintaining the slight bend in the elbows and control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping tension on the chest and avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: Seated lever crossovers are a machine chest fly variation that brings the arms together in front of the body to isolate and load the pectorals while minimizing torso movement. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to squeeze the chest at peak contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Assisted Standing Chin-up
exercises.notes: Set the machine assistance level (lighter assistance = harder) so you can perform controlled repetitions with good form. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the machine assistance level (lighter assistance = harder) so you can perform controlled repetitions with good form.
Stand on the platform facing the machine and grasp the handles with a supinated (underhand) grip about shoulder-width apart; arms should be fully extended at the start.
Keep feet planted on the platform, chest upright and core braced; avoid excessive swinging or leaning back.
Initiate the movement by pulling your elbows down and back, driving your chest toward the handles and squeezing the lats and upper back at the top of the motion.
Pause briefly with chin or upper chest close to the handles while maintaining scapular retraction and elbow flexion.
Slowly lower yourself back to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until they are fully extended.
Repeat for the desired number of reps and adjust assistance as needed to maintain strict form.
exercises.description: A lever-assisted, standing chin-up uses a counterbalance to reduce the effective bodyweight while performing an underhand pull, allowing you to train the vertical pulling pattern. It emphasizes the lats with strong involvement from the biceps and upper-back musculature for scapular retraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Rear Delt Row (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set the cable to a high pulley and attach a rope. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the cable to a high pulley and attach a rope. Stand facing the machine about 2–4 feet away and grab the rope with a neutral (thumbs-up) grip in both hands.
Adopt a staggered or shoulder-width stance with a slight knee bend and a small hip hinge so your torso is upright to slightly inclined forward; brace your core.
Step back until you feel constant tension on the cable, arms extended in front of you at about eye to forehead level.
Initiate the movement by externally rotating your shoulders and driving your elbows straight out to the sides (away from the body) while pulling the rope toward your upper chest/face.
At the end range, spread the rope ends apart slightly and squeeze the rear delts and upper back for 1–2 seconds; keep the elbows high and avoid shrugging.
Slowly reverse the movement under control, allowing the arms to extend forward until tension is maintained, but do not let the weight stack slam.
Breathe out during the pull (concentric) and inhale as you return (eccentric). Use a weight that allows strict form and full rear-delt activation.
Avoid leaning back or using torso momentum; if you cannot maintain elbows-high position, reduce the load or shorten the range of motion.
exercises.description: A standing cable row performed with a rope and a high pulley that emphasizes the posterior delts by pulling the rope toward the face/chest with elbows flared, hitting the rear shoulder fibers and upper back musculature.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Lever Decline Single Arm Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the machine seat/back so your torso is on a slight decline (head lower than hips) and select an appropriate weight on the lever. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the machine seat/back so your torso is on a slight decline (head lower than hips) and select an appropriate weight on the lever.
Sit with your back and head pressed firmly against the back pad, feet flat on the floor or on the machine footrests, and brace your core to maintain a neutral spine.
Grasp one handle with a neutral or slightly pronated grip; position your shoulder so the elbow starts roughly level with the lower chest.
Retract and stabilize your scapula (pull the shoulder blade slightly down and back) and keep your hips and torso from rotating throughout the set.
Exhale and press the lever down and forward in a controlled arc until the pressing arm is nearly extended — do not lock the elbow hard at the top.
Pause briefly at full extension to feel the pectoral contraction, then inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired number of reps on one side, then switch to the opposite arm and repeat, keeping the same setup and technique for both sides.
exercises.description: A unilateral decline-style chest press performed on a lever machine that emphasizes the lower portion of the pectorals while forcing core and shoulder stabilizers to work to keep the torso steady. Performed one arm at a time to correct imbalances and increase unilateral pressing strength.

exercises.name: Lever Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit roughly at mid-chest level when you grasp them; select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit roughly at mid-chest level when you grasp them; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back and head firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, wrists aligned with forearms and shoulders relaxed away from ears.
Begin with elbows slightly below shoulder height and forearms pointed forward (starting position).
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly together by extending your arms until nearly straight; avoid locking the elbows at full extension.
Pause briefly at the end of the press while maintaining control and keeping the chest engaged.
Inhale and slowly return the handles back to the starting position, controlling the weight and stopping when elbows reach roughly the same angle as the start.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping the torso stable and avoiding arching the lower back throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated lever chest press performed on a machine to push handles forward using the chest muscles; the movement also involves the front delts and triceps. Use a controlled pressing motion from mid-chest to near full arm extension without locking the elbows.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
Stand facing away from the machine and grab a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip; bring the handles to chest level with elbows bent and hands near the sternum.
Step forward into a staggered or shoulder-width stance so one foot is slightly in front of the other; brace your core and keep a slight bend in the knees.
Keep shoulders down and back, chest up, and press both handles forward and inward until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press; hold the end position briefly while keeping tension on the cables.
Inhale and slowly control the return of the handles back to the starting position at chest level, allowing the shoulder blades to set but not excessively retract.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding leaning forward or using excessive lower-back arching.
exercises.description: A standing cable press that targets the chest by pressing handles forward from chest level against cable resistance; the movement also engages the anterior delts and triceps while the core stabilizes the torso. Performed facing away from the cable column with a staggered stance for balance.

exercises.name: Lever T-bar Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding), and brace your core.
Begin with your arms extended and shoulders protracted so the weight is under control.
Exhale and pull the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular retraction and keeping the torso stable (avoid excessive torso lean or momentum).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start under control until arms are nearly extended, keeping tension in the back throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding shrugging the shoulders or rounding the lower back.
Adjust foot position or torso angle as needed to maintain a comfortable, neutral spine and full range of motion.
exercises.description: A bent-over plate-loaded T-bar row where you stand on the foot platform and hinge at the hips to pull the handle toward your torso, primarily working the lats and mid-back. The movement emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining a neutral spine.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Paused Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip (or mixed grip if needed for heavy loads) and brace your core.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together and driving your elbows back, bringing the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Hold and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1–2 seconds (the paused portion) while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excess torso rotation.
Lower the bar under control until arms are extended and the bar is near knee level or a natural hang, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for desired reps while breathing out during the pull and inhaling as you lower; avoid rounding the back or using excessive momentum.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed from a hip-hinged position with a brief pause at the top of the pull to maximize scapular retraction and time under tension. Emphasizes rowing the elbows back while keeping the torso stable and the spine neutral.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or overhand grip and the weights resting in front of your thighs.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement to protect the joint and avoid locking.
Exhale and raise both dumbbells straight in front of you to roughly shoulder height, leading with the hands and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause for a second at the top while maintaining upright posture and a tight core; do not lean backward or use hip drive.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement slow and controlled to minimize momentum and maximize delt activation.
If balance or form breaks down, reduce the weight or perform the raise one arm at a time.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell front raise variation where both dumbbells are lifted together in front of the body to work the anterior shoulder. Performed with a slight bend in the elbow and controlled tempo to isolate the delts and minimize momentum.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Wide Grip Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor; keep your spine neutral and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and place a loaded barbell on the floor in front of you.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor; keep your spine neutral and head in line with the spine.
Grip the bar with a wide overhand grip (wider than shoulder-width) and let your arms hang fully extended.
Brace your core, inhale, then pull the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows out and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders retracted, then exhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner until arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired repetitions while avoiding torso rotation, excessive momentum, or spinal rounding; keep knees slightly bent and maintain the hip hinge throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed with a wide overhand grip that emphasizes the upper back by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the bar toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. The torso stays hinged forward while the hips and spine stabilize the movement.

exercises.name: Landmine Squat and Press
exercises.notes: Set up the landmine by securing one end of the barbell in the pivot and load the free end with appropriate weight; stand facing the bar with feet about shoulder‑width apart and toes slightly turned out.
instructions: Set up the landmine by securing one end of the barbell in the pivot and load the free end with appropriate weight; stand facing the bar with feet about shoulder‑width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Grip the end of the bar with both hands and hold it close to your chest in a front‑rack/goblet position (elbows tucked under the bar, forearms vertical) while standing tall and bracing your abs.
Begin the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees to lower into a controlled squat until your thighs are roughly parallel to the floor or a comfortable depth is reached.

Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand up explosively; as you reach near full hip and knee extension, press the bar upward and slightly forward using your delts and triceps until your arms are extended.
Pause briefly at the top with a stable torso, then slowly lower the bar back to the chest/front‑rack position under control and immediately move into the next squat.
Maintain a neutral spine, keep the chest up, breathe in before the descent and exhale during the press, and avoid letting the knees cave inwards throughout the movement.
exercises.description: A front‑loaded compound movement performed with a landmine bar that combines a squat with an overhead/forward press, loading the quads during the descent and the shoulders/triceps during the press. It trains lower‑body strength and upper‑body pressing in a single fluid motion.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
