exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Hammer Grip Pull-up on Dip Cage
exercises.notes: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang.
instructions: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang. Cross or bend your legs behind you if needed to avoid touching the floor.
Brace your core and begin by depressing and retracting the shoulder blades (scapular pull) to engage the lats before bending the elbows.

Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back toward your ribs, leading with the chest until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the handles; keep the torso relatively upright and avoid excessive swinging or kipping.
Hold the top position briefly with shoulders packed (down and back), then slowly lower yourself under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended.
Breathe out as you pull up and inhale as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on lat contraction rather than momentum.
exercises.description: A neutral (hammer) grip pull-up performed on a dip/pull-up station that emphasizes the lats while reducing strain on the shoulders by using palms-facing-each-other handles. Performed from a dead hang and pulling the chest toward the bar with controlled descent.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Shoulder Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
Begin in a dead hang with arms fully extended, legs either straight or lightly crossed at the ankles, core braced and shoulders slightly depressed (scapulae down and back).
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down and back while squeezing the lats to lift your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar (or chest approaches the bar), keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging or kipping.
Lower yourself back to a full dead hang in a slow, controlled manner until arms are fully extended and scapulae are back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps — exhale during the upward (concentric) phase and inhale as you lower (eccentric) — maintain tension in the core and avoid shrugging the shoulders at the top.
exercises.description: A shoulder-width overhand pull-up where you hang from the bar and pull your body up using your back and arms. Focuses on vertical pulling to develop the lats while the biceps and upper back assist.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Hammer Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat so your back is fully supported on the incline pad and the handles start roughly at upper-chest / clavicle level when grasped.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat so your back is fully supported on the incline pad and the handles start roughly at upper-chest / clavicle level when grasped.
Select an appropriate weight and sit with your feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and your lower back gently pressed into the bench (avoid excessive arching).
Reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral (palms facing each other) 'hammer' grip; pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back to stabilize the chest.
Begin with elbows bent and positioned at about a 30–45° angle from the torso (not flared fully out), wrists neutral and aligned with forearms.
Exhale and press the handles upward and slightly together in a controlled motion until your arms are nearly extended — avoid locking the elbows at the top.
Pause briefly at the top and consciously squeeze the upper pectorals while maintaining scapular stability.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly back to the start position, keeping control of the weight and maintaining the same elbow angle and shoulder position.
Complete desired reps and return the lever to the safety/rest position before releasing the handles; avoid using momentum or excessive shoulder elevation during the set.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline lever press machine using a neutral (hammer) grip, this exercise drives the handles upward to target the upper chest. It emphasizes the upper pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps for pressing and stabilization.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single-handle attachment on a cable machine to a high pulley (above shoulder height).
instructions: Set a single-handle attachment on a cable machine to a high pulley (above shoulder height).
Stand facing across from the pulley in a staggered stance (one foot forward for balance) with a slight forward lean at the hips; engage your core and keep a neutral spine.
Reach up and grab the handle with the working hand using a neutral grip; allow the arm to start out to the side with a small bend in the elbow.
Depress and stabilize your shoulder (avoid shrugging) and pull the handle down and across your body in a smooth arc toward the opposite mid-chest, rotating the torso slightly as needed.
At the end of the movement, actively squeeze the pectorals and serratus anterior while maintaining the elbow bend; pause briefly at peak contraction.
Slowly and under control, return the handle to the starting position allowing the chest to stretch but keeping tension on the cable; do not let the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Keep breathing steadily (exhale as you pull across, inhale as you return).
Maintain a slight elbow bend throughout, avoid shoulder hyperextension, and do not use excessive torso rotation or momentum to drive the movement.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable crossover isolates one side of the chest by pulling a cable across the body from a high pulley, emphasizing the pectoral contraction and allowing a strong end-range squeeze. It is performed standing with a slight forward lean and a controlled return to the start position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back supported and feet flat on the floor for stability.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back supported and feet flat on the floor for stability.
Hold one dumbbell with both hands (palms cupped against the underside of the top plate) and extend your arms straight over your chest with a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core and keep your ribs down to maintain a neutral spine; do not hyperextend the lower back.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head by allowing your shoulders to extend, keeping the elbows slightly bent and fixed at that angle.
Lower until you feel a stretch in the lats/upper chest or until your range allows without shoulder discomfort.
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arc to the starting position over your chest by contracting the lats and driving the elbows down toward your hips.
Pause briefly at the top, maintain control on every rep, and avoid jerking the weight or flaring the ribs upward.
Perform repetitions with a controlled tempo; reduce weight if you cannot maintain a stable core and neutral spine.
exercises.description: Supine dumbbell pullover performed on a flat bench involves lowering a single dumbbell in an arc from over the chest behind the head to stretch and load the latissimus and then pulling it back over the chest. It emphasizes lat lengthening and shoulder extension while also engaging the chest, triceps and serratus anterior for control.

exercises.name: Close Grip Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with an underhand (supinated) close grip — hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower, thumbs wrapped around the bar.
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with an underhand (supinated) close grip — hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower, thumbs wrapped around the bar.

Start from a dead hang with arms fully extended, shoulders relaxed, torso vertical, legs either straight or lightly crossed at the ankles; brace your core and squeeze the glutes to prevent excessive swinging.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and driving the elbows down and back while pulling your chest toward the bar; focus on contracting the lats and pulling with the elbows rather than just the hands.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the bar, keeping the torso fairly upright and avoiding kipping or excessive leg drive.
Pause briefly at the top under control, then slowly lower yourself back to a full dead hang by extending the arms and allowing the shoulder blades to protract and depress.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Breathe out while pulling up and inhale while lowering. Maintain strict form and avoid momentum to maximize lat and biceps engagement.
exercises.description: A close-grip chin-up is a vertical pulling exercise performed from a dead hang with a narrow, underhand (supinated) grip that emphasizes the lats while placing increased demand on the biceps and forearms. It builds upper-back strength and arm pulling power.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

exercises.name: Cable High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.

Position an incline bench or chest pad in front of the machine so you can sit facing the pulleys with your chest pressed firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust pad height so torso is supported and arms can reach the handles overhead.
Sit tall with chest against the pad, neutral neck, core braced and shoulders down away from ears; reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with arms nearly extended overhead, scapulae slightly protracted; inhale to prepare.
Exhale and pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest by driving your elbows down and out, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end range.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds) while maintaining a supported chest and no torso rotation.
Slowly release the handles under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the start while keeping tension on the cables and scapulae engaged.
Repeat for desired reps; avoid using momentum, leaning away from the pad, or shrugging the shoulders upward.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported cable high row performed from dual high pulleys; you pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest while the chest rests against the pad to isolate the upper back. Emphasizes scapular retraction and posterior shoulder activation with reduced lower-back involvement.

exercises.name: Chest Dip (on dip pull-up cage)
exercises.notes: Start by standing between the parallel dip bars and grasp each handle firmly with a neutral grip; step or jump up so your arms are straight and you are supporting your bodyweight (feet can be bent behind you or crossed at the ankles).
instructions: Start by standing between the parallel dip bars and grasp each handle firmly with a neutral grip; step or jump up so your arms are straight and you are supporting your bodyweight (feet can be bent behind you or crossed at the ankles).

Lean your torso forward about 30–45 degrees (hinge at the hips) and lift your chest so your shoulders are slightly in front of your hands to place emphasis on the pectorals; keep your scapulae stable and shoulders down away from your ears.
Brace your core to prevent excessive swinging or arching of the lower back; maintain a controlled, steady body position throughout the set.

Slowly lower your body by bending the elbows, allowing them to move slightly outward (not excessively flared), until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or you reach about 90 degrees at the elbow.

Pause briefly at the bottom while keeping tension in the chest and triceps, then press yourself back up by extending the elbows and driving the chest forward to return to the start position; avoid forcefully locking the elbows.
Breathe in while lowering and exhale while pressing up; perform repetitions with a controlled tempo to maintain tension on the pectorals and reduce momentum.
Finish the set by stepping or lowering your feet to the ground in a controlled manner; if you experience shoulder pain, reduce range of motion or revert to a less forward-leaning dip variation.
exercises.description: A bodyweight dip performed on a dip/pull-up cage with the torso leaned forward to emphasize the chest; the movement lowers the body by bending the elbows and then presses it back up to extend the arms. It primarily targets the pectorals while heavily engaging the triceps and front delts for movement and the upper back for scapular stability.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a high pulley (above head height) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a high pulley (above head height) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on a bench facing the machine with your chest pressed firmly against the vertical chest support pad and feet flat on the floor for stability.
Reach up and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip; allow the working arm to start extended overhead while keeping your torso braced against the pad.
Engage your scapula (pull the shoulder blade slightly down and back) before initiating the pull to protect the shoulder.

Pull the handle downward and back in a controlled arc so the elbow travels behind your torso and the hand finishes near the upper chest/side, squeezing the lats and upper back at the end of the concentric phase.
Pause briefly at the contracted position while maintaining chest contact with the pad and avoiding torso rotation or leaning.
Slowly return the handle to the start position with control, allowing the arm to extend fully while maintaining scapular control.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Keep breathing: exhale while pulling, inhale while returning.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm high cable row performed with the chest braced against a vertical pad; pull the handle down and back to drive the elbow behind the torso and load the lats and upper back. This isolates one side for focused unilateral strength and scapular retraction.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Wide grip Row
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
instructions: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
Start with arms extended, torso upright and chest up, maintaining a neutral spine and slight forward lean from the hips.
Initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction), then drive the elbows out and back to bring the bar toward the upper abdomen/lower chest.
Squeeze the upper back and hold the contracted position for 1 second, keeping the neck neutral and avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum.
Slowly return the bar to the start by extending the arms under control while maintaining an engaged core and neutral spine; inhale on the return, exhale during the pull.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding jerking or using the lower back to complete the pull.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip rows train the upper back by pulling a wide handle toward the torso, emphasizing the mid-back and rear shoulder muscles while minimizing lower-back involvement. Use controlled scapular retraction and elbow drive to maximize upper-back engagement.

exercises.name: Lever Bent over Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (approximately 30–45°), keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly up.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and position yourself in front of the plate-loaded lever so the bar/handle is within reach.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (approximately 30–45°), keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly up.
Grasp the bar with a shoulder-width (or slightly narrower) pronated grip and brace your core to prevent rounding of the lower back.
Initiate the rep by pulling your elbows straight back, driving the bar toward your lower ribcage/hip area while retracting and squeezing the shoulder blades.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive upper-trap shrugging.
Slowly lower the bar under control until your arms are nearly extended and tension is felt in the lats, then prepare for the next rep.
Keep breaths controlled (exhale on the pull, inhale on the return) and maintain a neutral spine throughout; reduce weight if you cannot keep proper hip-hinge form.
exercises.description: A plate-loaded lever version of the bent-over row where you hinge at the hips and pull the bar/handle toward your torso, emphasizing horizontal pulling to develop the lats and mid/upper back. Maintain a neutral spine and stable core while driving elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades.

exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and your head and upper back supported; grip the barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and your head and upper back supported; grip the barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart.
Start with the barbell held directly over your chest with your arms nearly extended and a slight bend in the elbows; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc backwards and over your head until you feel a stretch through the chest and lats (keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the motion).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover, then pull the bar back in the same arc to bring it over your chest by contracting your lats and pectorals.
From the chest position, press the barbell straight up overhead until arms are nearly extended to complete the press portion, then lower the bar back to the chest in control.
Repeat the pullover-to-press sequence for the desired reps; maintain core tension, avoid overarching the lower back, and use a spotter for heavier loads.
exercises.description: A supine barbell movement that combines a pullover (bar travels in an arc behind the head) with a press back to overhead, emphasizing a stretch and contraction of the chest while also recruiting the lats and triceps.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
instructions: Sit on the machine seat with knees bent and feet planted on the footrests; brace your chest against the chest pad (if present) and sit upright.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
Set your spine neutral, retract your shoulder blades slightly, and maintain a tall chest—do not round your back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back, pulling the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and feel the lats and mid-back engage; avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start position in a controlled manner until your arms are nearly straight and the lats are stretched.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping consistent tempo, full range of motion, and a firm grip throughout.
exercises.description: Seated pronated-grip lever row performed on a plate-loaded machine to develop the lats and mid-upper back by pulling a lever toward the torso using an overhand grip. Emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining an upright, braced seated position.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Bench Press on Floor
exercises.notes: Set a barbell on a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on the floor; have a spotter if available.
instructions: Set a barbell on a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on the floor; have a spotter if available.
Lie supine (on your back) on the floor with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor to stabilize your hips and lower back.
Reach up and grasp the bar with a reverse (underhand/supinated) grip, hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower; wrists should be neutral and stacked over the forearms.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving it directly over your chest with elbows locked and shoulders relaxed.

Inhale, then slowly lower the bar until your elbows touch or come just above the floor (upper arms at or slightly below parallel to the floor), keeping your elbows tucked slightly toward your torso to protect the shoulders.
Pause briefly with control, then exhale and press the bar upward until your arms are fully extended without locking the shoulders aggressively.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a steady, controlled tempo; keep your back flat on the floor (avoid excessive arching) and keep the wrists straight.
When finished, carefully rack the bar with the help of a spotter or by guiding it back onto the rack supports.
exercises.description: A floor-based bench press performed with a reverse (underhand/supinated) grip that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while limiting range of motion. Lying on the floor, you lower the bar to the chest/upper torso and press it back up under control.

exercises.name: Cable Rope Seated Row
exercises.notes: Grasp the rope attachment with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and sit upright with a neutral spine and chest up; start with arms fully extended.
instructions: Sit on a seated row bench or platform with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the rope attachment with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and sit upright with a neutral spine and chest up; start with arms fully extended.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) and drive your elbows straight back toward your torso.
Pull the rope toward your upper abdomen or lower chest, keeping elbows close to the body and leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Pause briefly at peak contraction while squeezing the lats and upper back, maintaining an upright torso (avoid excessive trunk lean or momentum).
Slowly extend the arms back to the starting position with control, returning the shoulders to a slightly protracted position while keeping the spine neutral.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, ensuring wrists remain neutral and movements are smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated cable rope row performed from a bench using a rope attachment to pull the handle toward the torso, emphasizing scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the lats and upper back. Keeps a neutral spine and controlled tempo to isolate the pulling muscles.

exercises.name: Lever High Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with feet flat on the floor and chest/torso stabilized against the pad (or chest pad if present).
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with feet flat on the floor and chest/torso stabilized against the pad (or chest pad if present). Adjust seat so handles are at or slightly above shoulder height when arms are extended.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, arms extended and shoulders relaxed. Take a full breath and brace your core.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the handles down and back toward the upper chest/near clavicle, leading with the elbows and keeping them at roughly shoulder height (elbows flared).
Squeeze the shoulder blades and upper back at the end of the concentric phase for 1 second, keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive trunk lean or momentum.
Slowly extend the arms back to the start position under control (eccentric phase), maintaining tension in the upper back and preventing the weights from slamming.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out during the pull and inhale during the release.

Common form tips: keep the neck neutral, avoid shrugging the shoulders up, don’t use excessive body swing, and adjust seat/handle height so the pull targets the upper back rather than purely the lats.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded high row where you pull overhead/forward handles toward the upper chest to emphasize the upper back (rhomboids, mid traps and rear delts) while also engaging lats and arm muscles. The torso is stabilized against a pad and movement is performed in a controlled pull-and-release.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying One Arm Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand at chest level with a firm grip (palm facing forward or neutral); place your free hand on your chest or the bench for balance.
Retract your shoulder blade slightly on the working side and brace your core to prevent your torso from rotating.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight up until the arm is nearly extended, keeping a soft bend in the elbow (do not fully lock out).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell under control back to the chest level, keeping the elbow at about a 45-degree angle relative to the torso.
Maintain steady posture and avoid twisting; reset your scapula and core between reps if needed.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat with equal control and setup.
exercises.description: A single-arm dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a flat bench to train the pectorals unilaterally while the triceps and delts assist; the opposite hand rests for stability. Emphasizes controlled pressing and core stabilization to prevent torso rotation.

exercises.name: Cable Seated One Arm Alternate Row
exercises.notes: Step 1: Set a single handle on a low pulley and sit on a bench facing the machine with feet flat and slightly braced on the floor or foot platform.
instructions: Step 1: Set a single handle on a low pulley and sit on a bench facing the machine with feet flat and slightly braced on the floor or foot platform.

Step 2: Sit upright with a neutral spine, chest slightly up, shoulders relaxed, and core braced; reach forward to grab the handle with one hand while the opposite hand can rest on your thigh for stability.
Step 3: Start with the working arm fully extended and the shoulder gently protracted (forward) so there is mild tension in the cable.
Step 4: Pull the handle back by driving the elbow straight toward your hip, focusing on squeezing the shoulder blade toward the spine (scapular retraction) and keeping the elbow close to your body.
Step 5: Maintain a stable torso — avoid rotating or leaning excessively; use the arm and back to move the weight, not body momentum.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the end of the concentric phase with a full squeeze of the lats and upper back, then slowly return the handle to the starting position under control.
Step 7: Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning. Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat, alternating sides for each set.
exercises.description: A seated unilateral cable row that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi through a controlled elbow-driven pull while promoting scapular retraction; performed alternating one arm at a time to address side-to-side imbalances.

exercises.name: Lever One Arm Lateral High Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your chest braced against the pad, feet flat on the floor, core engaged and torso stable to prevent twisting.
Reach up and grasp the single high handle with one hand (neutral or pronated grip) while keeping the shoulder down and away from the ear.
Initiate the movement by driving the elbow down and back in a lateral arc, leading with the elbow so the hand moves toward the side of your ribs and the shoulder blade retracts and rotates downward.
Squeeze the upper back (scapulae) at the end range for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation or rotation.
Slowly return the handle to the start position with control, fully extending the arm while maintaining tension in the working muscles.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat. Maintain steady breathing (exhale while pulling, inhale while returning).
exercises.description: A single-arm high pulley/lever row performed from a seated position that pulls the handle laterally and upward to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder musculature. It focuses on scapular retraction and elbow-led pulling to target the upper back and lats while the arm and grip work the biceps and forearms.

exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Larsen Press
exercises.notes: Set a barbell in a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on a flat bench; load appropriate weight and use a spotter or safety pins.
instructions: Set a barbell in a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on a flat bench; load appropriate weight and use a spotter or safety pins.
Lie supine on the bench with your eyes roughly under the bar, feet planted flat on the floor for stability, and shoulder blades squeezed together.
Grip the bar with a close/narrow grip (hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower) with thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Unrack the bar and hold it with arms extended over the sternum, wrists stacked over elbows and bar positioned vertically above your chest.
Lower the bar under control to the upper-to-mid chest (sternal/clavicular area) while keeping elbows tucked closer to your body to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to lightly rest on the chest (brief pause) — this is the Larsen touch-and-rest — then drive the bar upward by pressing through the chest and triceps until arms are nearly extended.
Keep a tight torso and steady breathing: inhale on the descent, exhale as you press up. Repeat for desired reps.
Re‑rack the bar under control at the end of the set, making sure the bar is fully seated on the rack supports.
exercises.description: A close‑grip variation of the bench press where the bar is briefly rested on the chest (Larsen style) between reps; emphasizes the inner chest while also heavily recruiting the triceps and front delts. Performed lying supine on a flat bench using a narrow hand position.

exercises.name: Cable Close Grip Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the pads so your torso cannot rise during the movement (seated position).
Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
Start with arms extended overhead, chest up and a slight lean backward from the hips (about 10–20°) to put tension on the lats (slight recline).
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows down and back, leading with the elbows rather than the hands, and pull the bar to the upper chest/upper sternum.
At the bottom of the movement, squeeze your lats and upper back for a brief 1–2 second pause while keeping the torso stable and not using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start in a controlled manner until your lats are under tension again (full but not locked extension).
Repeat for the desired number of reps; keep core engaged and avoid excessive swinging or pulling with the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a close grip to emphasize the latissimus dorsi while minimizing shoulder abduction; a narrow grip increases biceps and upper-back involvement. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handle to the upper chest.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Standing Upright Row
exercises.notes: Stand tall (upright) with feet about hip-width apart and knees soft; hold an EZ bar in front of your thighs with a pronated (overhand) narrow to shoulder-width grip.
instructions: Stand tall (upright) with feet about hip-width apart and knees soft; hold an EZ bar in front of your thighs with a pronated (overhand) narrow to shoulder-width grip.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back, and keep your chest up and spine neutral throughout the movement.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows upward and outward, keeping the bar close to the body and leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Raise the bar to approximately upper chest / lower clavicle height (or until elbows reach around shoulder level), keeping wrists relatively neutral to avoid excessive bending.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged but without hyperextending or shrugging excessively.
Lower the bar slowly and under control back to the starting position, fully extending the arms but maintaining tension in the shoulders.
Breathe out as you lift the bar and inhale as you lower it; avoid using momentum or excessive torso lean to move the weight.
Use a lighter weight and a slightly wider grip if you experience shoulder impingement; stop if you feel sharp shoulder pain.
exercises.description: A standing upright row performed with an EZ bar that elevates the bar toward the upper chest, emphasizing the lateral delts and upper traps while keeping the torso upright. Use a close grip and lead with the elbows to maximize shoulder activation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Seated Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (seated position); keep your chest up and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (seated position); keep your chest up and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent about 90 degrees and palms facing forward or slightly inward (neutral grip); keep wrists stacked over forearms.
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid excessive lower-back arching by tightening your abs and keeping the ribcage down.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly straight overhead but not fully locked out.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stabilized (do not shrug up toward your ears), then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to shoulder height.
Keep the movement controlled, elbows tracking under the wrists, and avoid letting the dumbbells drift too far forward or backward.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, focusing on steady tempo and maintaining an upright seated position throughout.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell press performed from a bench, pressing dumbbells overhead to work the shoulder muscles. The movement emphasizes the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper chest for stabilization and extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Arnold Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent and palms facing your body (supinated position).
Keep your elbows slightly in front of your torso and maintain a neutral spine with minimal lower-back arch.
Exhale and press the dumbbells upward while rotating your wrists so the palms face forward at the top; fully extend the arms without locking the elbows aggressively.
Pause briefly at full extension with the weights overhead and shoulders stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down while rotating the wrists to return the palms to face your body and the weights to shoulder height.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping the movement controlled and avoiding excessive trunk lean or using momentum.
exercises.description: A seated dumbbell variation where you start with palms facing you at shoulder height and rotate the wrists while pressing the weights overhead; primarily develops the shoulder (delts) with pressing and rotational motion.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip and take a hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees.
Hinge forward at the hips and place your forehead or chest gently against the bench pad for support so your torso is close to horizontal (bench-assisted bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep a neutral spine and the head supported on the bench.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc — lead with the elbows — until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and you feel the rear delts contract.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position.
Keep the movement strict (no torso rotation or momentum) and maintain head/chest support on the bench throughout the set.
Choose a weight that allows controlled form for the full range of motion and perform recommended reps.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell raise that targets the rear (posterior) delts by moving the arms out to the sides while the torso is stabilized. The head/chest is supported on an incline bench to reduce momentum and isolate the rear deltoids.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Band Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.
instructions: Stand upright with feet together or hip-width apart, step on the middle of the band to anchor it securely under both feet.
Hold a handle in each hand with palms facing your body and arms hanging at your sides; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your abs and keep your torso tall and still; avoid leaning or using momentum.
Exhale and lift both arms out to the sides leading with the elbows until your hands reach shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep wrists neutral and maintain the slight elbow bend throughout the movement; do not shrug the shoulders upward.
Pause briefly at the top to feel the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the arms back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for desired reps, maintaining smooth, controlled movement and stable posture on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing lateral raise using a resistance band where you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height to emphasize the lateral delts. The band provides continuous tension while the traps and forearms assist for stability and grip.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline One Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to an incline of about 30–45 degrees and sit so the side of your torso is supported by the bench (chest/side against the pad) with feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to an incline of about 30–45 degrees and sit so the side of your torso is supported by the bench (chest/side against the pad) with feet planted on the floor.

Hold a dumbbell in the working hand with a neutral grip (palm facing your body) and let the arm hang straight down at your side with a very slight bend in the elbow; keep your torso pressed into the bench to prevent cheating.
Brace your core, keep your neck neutral, and exhale as you raise the dumbbell out to the side in a controlled arc until your upper arm is roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep the elbow slightly bent and avoid rotating the shoulder or shrugging—lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist, to emphasize the delts and limit trap involvement.
Pause for a brief second at the top with tension on the deltoid, then inhale and slowly lower the weight back to the start position under control.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch sides; choose a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if the traps begin to dominate.
exercises.description: Performed lying/leaning on an incline bench and raising one dumbbell out to the side, this unilateral lateral raise isolates the lateral head of the deltoid to build shoulder width. The bench support reduces torso movement so the working shoulder does most of the work.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Cuban Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body).
Begin the movement by driving your elbows up and out in a controlled upright-row motion, keeping the dumbbells close to the body until your elbows reach roughly shoulder height.

With elbows elevated, externally rotate at the shoulders so your forearms move forward and the dumbbells are brought in front of your face/chest area (upper arms should be roughly parallel to the floor).
From that externally rotated position, press the dumbbells straight overhead until your arms are fully extended, keeping the core tight and avoiding excessive back arch.

Lower the dumbbells back down to the externally rotated position in a controlled manner, then rotate the forearms back to the upright-row position and lower the weights to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, using a weight that allows smooth, controlled rotation and full overhead lockout without shrugging the shoulders.
exercises.description: A standing compound shoulder movement that combines an upright row, external rotation, and overhead press to build deltoid strength and shoulder stability while recruiting the traps and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Curl to Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides in a neutral grip (palms facing inward).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides in a neutral grip (palms facing inward).

Initiate a controlled dumbbell curl by rotating the palms to face upward and bending the elbows to bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level; keep elbows close to your sides and avoid swinging the torso.

At the top of the curl with dumbbells near shoulder height, rotate palms so they face forward and press the weights overhead by extending the elbows and driving through the shoulders until arms are fully extended.
Pause briefly at full extension with the ribs down and core engaged; avoid overarching the lower back.
Lower the dumbbells back down in a controlled motion to shoulder height, then reverse the curl movement to return the weights to your sides in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the press and inhaling as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and avoid using momentum from the hips or legs.
exercises.description: A standing combination exercise that starts with a dumbbell curl and transitions into an overhead press, targeting the shoulder muscles while also working the arms. Perform the curl to bring weights to shoulder level, then press the dumbbells overhead and control the descent back to the start.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
